APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it addresses lexical entailment in context (Sections 1–3), proposes practical contextualized representations and similarity features (Sections 3–4), and introduces two evaluation datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN in Section 5) with monolingual and cross-lingual experiments (Section 6–7). The methods, baselines, metrics (weighted F1) and analyses (Section 8) follow community standards, and the paper is well-positioned against prior work (Turney et al., Baroni et al., Shwartz & Dagan), even reporting state-of-the-art results—making it a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue. | B(5): This is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it addresses lexical semantics and contextualized word representations (Sections 3–4) and introduces new datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN) for rigorous evaluation (Section 5). The problem formulation—lexical entailment grounded in exemplar contexts—is well targeted to the venue’s audience, and the paper uses community-expected methods and standards (GloVe/Context2Vec/BiVec embeddings, cross-lingual transfer, logistic regression baselines, weighted F1, and train/dev/test splits) with direct comparisons to prior work and a reported SOTA improvement (Section 7). Positioning and literature integration are appropriate, citing relevant prior work and motivating contributions across monolingual and cross-lingual settings.

reviewer_a.justification: This is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it addresses lexical entailment in context (Sections 1–3), proposes practical contextualized representations and similarity features (Sections 3–4), and introduces two evaluation datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN in Section 5) with monolingual and cross-lingual experiments (Section 6–7). The methods, baselines, metrics (weighted F1) and analyses (Section 8) follow community standards, and the paper is well-positioned against prior work (Turney et al., Baroni et al., Shwartz & Dagan), even reporting state-of-the-art results—making it a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it addresses lexical semantics and contextualized word representations (Sections 3–4) and introduces new datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN) for rigorous evaluation (Section 5). The problem formulation—lexical entailment grounded in exemplar contexts—is well targeted to the venue’s audience, and the paper uses community-expected methods and standards (GloVe/Context2Vec/BiVec embeddings, cross-lingual transfer, logistic regression baselines, weighted F1, and train/dev/test splits) with direct comparisons to prior work and a reported SOTA improvement (Section 7). Positioning and literature integration are appropriate, citing relevant prior work and motivating contributions across monolingual and cross-lingual settings.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of lexical entailment grounded in context (motivated in Introduction) and introduces two evaluation resources including a large automatic CONTEXT-WN dataset (Section 5), which materially facilitates study of contextualized entailment. Its proposed contextualized representations and similarity features yield substantial empirical gains (e.g. ~14-point improvement over a type-based baseline and a +4.8 F1 improvement over prior SOTA on CONTEXT-PPDB; Section 7) and generalize cross-lingually (Section 6.2/7.3). These contributions are likely to influence future work and be built upon (datasets, features, cross-lingual transfer), though they are evolutionary rather than fully transformative for the broader field. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of lexical entailment in context (motivated by QA examples) and demonstrates that contextualized word representations and word-context similarity features yield substantial gains (e.g., ~14 point improvement over context-agnostic baselines and a ~4.8 F1 improvement over prior SOTA; Sections 3,7). It introduces two novel evaluation datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN; Section 5) and shows cross-lingual generalization (BiVec/BiCVM gains of 6–10 points; Section 7.3), indicating the methods are broadly applicable. By explicitly modeling sense and entailment directionality (Section 8) and relying on features induced from raw text, the work is likely to influence follow-up research on contextual lexical semantics and practical NLU tasks, though it is an incremental methodological advance rather than a paradigm shift.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of lexical entailment grounded in context (motivated in Introduction) and introduces two evaluation resources including a large automatic CONTEXT-WN dataset (Section 5), which materially facilitates study of contextualized entailment. Its proposed contextualized representations and similarity features yield substantial empirical gains (e.g. ~14-point improvement over a type-based baseline and a +4.8 F1 improvement over prior SOTA on CONTEXT-PPDB; Section 7) and generalize cross-lingually (Section 6.2/7.3). These contributions are likely to influence future work and be built upon (datasets, features, cross-lingual transfer), though they are evolutionary rather than fully transformative for the broader field.

It introduces two novel evaluation datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN; Section 5) and shows cross-lingual generalization (BiVec/BiCVM gains of 6–10 points; Section 7.3), indicating the methods are broadly applicable. By explicitly modeling sense and entailment directionality (Section 8) and relying on features induced from raw text, the work is likely to influence follow-up research on contextual lexical semantics and practical NLU tasks, though it is an incremental methodological advance rather than a paradigm shift.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper advances a clear new perspective by explicitly framing lexical entailment as an in-context problem (vs prior out-of-context work such as Turney & Mohammad) and instantiates this with novel datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB augmentation and the large automatically-derived CONTEXT-WN). Methodologically it combines existing components (Context2Vec, contextual masking, similarity features) but adapts and empirically validates a specific masked min/max/mean contextualization and cross-lingual transfer for entailment, which is a non-trivial extension over prior contextualization work (e.g. Erk & Padó, Thater et al.) rather than a mere replication. Overall the contribution is a notable, well-differentiated step (datasets + context-aware modeling) rather than a purely incremental tweak. | B(3): The paper reframes lexical entailment as an in-context task, introduces a large automatic CONTEXT-WN dataset (Sec.5) and applies contextualized/masked word representations and word–context similarity features (Sec.3–4) to entailment detection, including cross-lingual evaluation (Sec.6–7). However, the core technical ideas (contextualizing type embeddings via masks/summary statistics, use of Context2Vec, and element-wise context filters) closely follow prior work (Erk & Pado 2008; Thater et al. 2011; Melamud et al. 2016), so the contribution is mainly an empirical adaptation and dataset/benchmark novelty rather than a fundamentally new method. Thus the paper is a moderate (incremental) novelty contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper advances a clear new perspective by explicitly framing lexical entailment as an in-context problem (vs prior out-of-context work such as Turney & Mohammad) and instantiates this with novel datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB augmentation and the large automatically-derived CONTEXT-WN). Methodologically it combines existing components (Context2Vec, contextual masking, similarity features) but adapts and empirically validates a specific masked min/max/mean contextualization and cross-lingual transfer for entailment, which is a non-trivial extension over prior contextualization work (e.g. Erk & Padó, Thater et al.) rather than a mere replication. Overall the contribution is a notable, well-differentiated step (datasets + context-aware modeling) rather than a purely incremental tweak.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper reframes lexical entailment as an in-context task, introduces a large automatic CONTEXT-WN dataset (Sec.5) and applies contextualized/masked word representations and word–context similarity features (Sec.3–4) to entailment detection, including cross-lingual evaluation (Sec.6–7). However, the core technical ideas (contextualizing type embeddings via masks/summary statistics, use of Context2Vec, and element-wise context filters) closely follow prior work (Erk & Pado 2008; Thater et al. 2011; Melamud et al. 2016), so the contribution is mainly an empirical adaptation and dataset/benchmark novelty rather than a fundamentally new method. Thus the paper is a moderate (incremental) novelty contribution.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): Overall the paper is organized in a standard logical order (Introduction → representation methods in Section 3 → comparison and classifier in Section 4 → datasets and experiments in Sections 5–7 → analysis/conclusion) and the main contributions (contextualizing word-type embeddings and new CONTEXT-PPDB/CONTEXT-WN datasets) are stated early in the Introduction. Notation such as ~wl, Cl and the Context2Vec representations is introduced and usable, but the manuscript contains many presentation issues (repeated paragraphs, stray LaTeX/numbering artifacts like repeated lines and misplaced subsection markers, and references to Table/Figure numbers without the visuals in the text excerpt) that reduce readability. Examples (e.g., the chess/game contexts) are effective, but inconsistent formatting and occasional truncations/typos make some sections hard to follow and interrupt the flow. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow (Introduction → representation methods in Section 3 → comparison and classifier in Section 4 → datasets and experimental setup in Sections 5–6 → results and analysis in Sections 7–8). The main contributions (contextualized word representations, similarity features, and new datasets) are stated early in the introduction and reiterated in the conclusion. Notation is mostly consistent (e.g. ~w_l, C_l, Context2Vec representations), and the concrete example (chess/game) effectively motivates the work; however the provided text contains presentation glitches — repeated paragraphs, some formatting artifacts, and an unclear expression (“F = ~60”) — and several figures/tables are only referenced (Table 2, Figure 2, Table 4/5) rather than visible, which slightly impairs readability.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is organized in a standard logical order (Introduction → representation methods in Section 3 → comparison and classifier in Section 4 → datasets and experiments in Sections 5–7 → analysis/conclusion) and the main contributions (contextualizing word-type embeddings and new CONTEXT-PPDB/CONTEXT-WN datasets) are stated early in the Introduction. Notation such as ~wl, Cl and the Context2Vec representations is introduced and usable, but the manuscript contains many presentation issues (repeated paragraphs, stray LaTeX/numbering artifacts like repeated lines and misplaced subsection markers, and references to Table/Figure numbers without the visuals in the text excerpt) that reduce readability. Examples (e.g., the chess/game contexts) are effective, but inconsistent formatting and occasional truncations/typos make some sections hard to follow and interrupt the flow.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow (Introduction → representation methods in Section 3 → comparison and classifier in Section 4 → datasets and experimental setup in Sections 5–6 → results and analysis in Sections 7–8). The main contributions (contextualized word representations, similarity features, and new datasets) are stated early in the introduction and reiterated in the conclusion. Notation is mostly consistent (e.g. ~w_l, C_l, Context2Vec representations), and the concrete example (chess/game) effectively motivates the work; however the provided text contains presentation glitches — repeated paragraphs, some formatting artifacts, and an unclear expression (“F = ~60”) — and several figures/tables are only referenced (Table 2, Figure 2, Table 4/5) rather than visible, which slightly impairs readability.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper directly compares to standard context-agnostic baselines (Baroni et al. 2012; Turney & Mohammad 2013; Roller et al. 2014) and to the prior state-of-the-art for relations-in-context (Shwartz & Dagan 2015), and reports improvements (e.g. +4.8 F1 on CONTEXT-PPDB). It uses the same classifier family and evaluation metric (logistic regression, weighted F1) “to allow for direct comparisons with previous work,” performs sensible ablations (Context2Vec vs masked/context-mean variants), and discusses related modeling choices (Erk & Pado, Thater et al., Dinu & Lapata), showing fair and comprehensive comparisons under consistent conditions. | B(5): The paper compares against appropriate, recent baselines (Baroni et al. 2012; Turney & Mohammad 2013; Roller et al. 2014; Shwartz & Dagan 2015) and establishes a new state-of-the-art over Shwartz & Dagan (Section 7). Comparisons are carried out under fair conditions using the same datasets/splits and weighted F1 (Section 6), the same classifier family (logistic regression) and limited parameter tuning. The authors cite and discuss relevant contextualization and sense-aware methods (Erk & Padó, Thater et al., Melamud et al.) and perform analyses/ablations (Context2Vec behavior, masked vs. mean contexts) that acknowledge when prior components perform comparably or worse (Sections 7 and 8).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper directly compares to standard context-agnostic baselines (Baroni et al. 2012; Turney & Mohammad 2013; Roller et al. 2014) and to the prior state-of-the-art for relations-in-context (Shwartz & Dagan 2015), and reports improvements (e.g. +4.8 F1 on CONTEXT-PPDB). It uses the same classifier family and evaluation metric (logistic regression, weighted F1) “to allow for direct comparisons with previous work,” performs sensible ablations (Context2Vec vs masked/context-mean variants), and discusses related modeling choices (Erk & Pado, Thater et al., Dinu & Lapata), showing fair and comprehensive comparisons under consistent conditions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate, recent baselines (Baroni et al. 2012; Turney & Mohammad 2013; Roller et al. 2014; Shwartz & Dagan 2015) and establishes a new state-of-the-art over Shwartz & Dagan (Section 7). Comparisons are carried out under fair conditions using the same datasets/splits and weighted F1 (Section 6), the same classifier family (logistic regression) and limited parameter tuning. The authors cite and discuss relevant contextualization and sense-aware methods (Erk & Padó, Thater et al., Melamud et al.) and perform analyses/ablations (Context2Vec behavior, masked vs. mean contexts) that acknowledge when prior components perform comparably or worse (Sections 7 and 8).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims (contextualized word representations and word–context similarity features improve lexical entailment in context) are clearly stated and supported by experiments reporting substantial F1 gains over context-agnostic baselines and prior SOTA (Section 7). Mathematical content is minimal and correct; the experimental setup uses reasonable baselines (Baroni et al., Shwartz & Dagan), pretrained embeddings (GloVe, Context2Vec, BiVec/BiCVM), and disjoint train/dev/test splits to avoid memorization (Section 5–6). Minor issues include under-specified dataset construction details (how hypernym synsets are chosen, possible label noise from the automatic negative sampling in CONTEXT-WN and an inconsistent reporting of dataset counts), and omission of statistical significance testing and some hyperparameter details, but these do not undermine the reported empirical improvements. Overall the conclusions are supported by the presented results despite these reporting gaps. | B(4): The main claim—that contextualized word representations and word-context similarity features improve lexical-entailment detection—is clearly stated and empirically supported by experiments on two new datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN), cross-lingual transfer, and ablations (Sections 5–8). Mathematical content is light and correct for the methods used (masking, pooled context statistics, logistic regression). The experimental setup uses reasonable baselines (GloVe, Context2Vec, prior PPDB features), controlled splits (different word pairs per split) and ablations, but the paper omits statistical significance testing and provides limited validation of the automatically generated CONTEXT-WN negatives (potential noise/artifacts), which are minor methodological concerns that do not overturn the reported gains. Overall the conclusions are supported by the presented results, with small caveats about dataset construction and absence of significance measures.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims (contextualized word representations and word–context similarity features improve lexical entailment in context) are clearly stated and supported by experiments reporting substantial F1 gains over context-agnostic baselines and prior SOTA (Section 7). Mathematical content is minimal and correct; the experimental setup uses reasonable baselines (Baroni et al., Shwartz & Dagan), pretrained embeddings (GloVe, Context2Vec, BiVec/BiCVM), and disjoint train/dev/test splits to avoid memorization (Section 5–6). Minor issues include under-specified dataset construction details (how hypernym synsets are chosen, possible label noise from the automatic negative sampling in CONTEXT-WN and an inconsistent reporting of dataset counts), and omission of statistical significance testing and some hyperparameter details, but these do not undermine the reported empirical improvements. Overall the conclusions are supported by the presented results despite these reporting gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claim—that contextualized word representations and word-context similarity features improve lexical-entailment detection—is clearly stated and empirically supported by experiments on two new datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB, CONTEXT-WN), cross-lingual transfer, and ablations (Sections 5–8). Mathematical content is light and correct for the methods used (masking, pooled context statistics, logistic regression). The experimental setup uses reasonable baselines (GloVe, Context2Vec, prior PPDB features), controlled splits (different word pairs per split) and ablations, but the paper omits statistical significance testing and provides limited validation of the automatically generated CONTEXT-WN negatives (potential noise/artifacts), which are minor methodological concerns that do not overturn the reported gains. Overall the conclusions are supported by the presented results, with small caveats about dataset construction and absence of significance measures.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates its claims across multiple complementary benchmarks (two novel datasets CONTEXT-PPDB and CONTEXT-WN, plus a cross-lingual transfer set) and reports comparisons to prior baselines and a prior state-of-the-art, showing consistent gains. It includes several ablations and sensitivity analyses (effects of context-aware similarities, masked vs mean contextualization, use of Context2Vec, PPDB-specific features, Macro-F1 analysis for context sensitivity) supporting the proposed design choices. Related work is surveyed and positioned against prior contextualization and lexical-entailment methods. However, explicit failure-case analysis and discussion of limitations is limited, and some implementation specifics for the masked-context construction and other low-level reproduction details are not fully specified in the provided text (though common resources and embedding choices are reported). | B(4): The paper presents substantial experiments: evaluations on two new in-context datasets (CONTEXT-PPDB and large-scale CONTEXT-WN, Section 5), monolingual and cross-lingual settings (Section 6), and compares to strong baselines/state-of-the-art (Section 7). It includes useful ablations/sensitivity analyses (representation-only vs context-aware features, masked vs mean context variants, Context2Vec usage, and a Macro-F1 context-sensitivity analysis in Section 8), and reports some negative findings (e.g. poor performance using ~c_c2v alone). However, discussion of failure cases and limitations is limited and some reproduction details (full training hyperparameters, seeds, code release timing) are not fully specified, so while thorough overall there remain minor gaps for complete reproducibility and error analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates its claims across multiple complementary benchmarks (two novel datasets CONTEXT-PPDB and CONTEXT-WN, plus a cross-lingual transfer set) and reports comparisons to prior baselines and a prior state-of-the-art, showing consistent gains. It includes several ablations and sensitivity analyses (effects of context-aware similarities, masked vs mean contextualization, use of Context2Vec, PPDB-specific features, Macro-F1 analysis for context sensitivity) supporting the proposed design choices. Related work is surveyed and positioned against prior contextualization and lexical-entailment methods. However, explicit failure-case analysis and discussion of limitations is limited, and some implementation specifics for the masked-context construction and other low-level reproduction details are not fully specified in the provided text (though common resources and embedding choices are reported).

It includes useful ablations/sensitivity analyses (representation-only vs context-aware features, masked vs mean context variants, Context2Vec usage, and a Macro-F1 context-sensitivity analysis in Section 8), and reports some negative findings (e.g. poor performance using ~c_c2v alone). However, discussion of failure cases and limitations is limited and some reproduction details (full training hyperparameters, seeds, code release timing) are not fully specified, so while thorough overall there remain minor gaps for complete reproducibility and error analysis.
